title: Justin Rose's Masters Miracle: The 45-Year-Old Storming Into the Lead
excerpt: In a stunning turn of events on Masters Sunday, 45-year-old Justin Rose has surged into the lead at Augusta National, proving age is just a number.
body:
Rose's Resurrection

Sunday at Augusta National, and the script nobody expected is writing itself. At 45 years old, Justin Rose has clawed his way into the lead at the 2026 Masters, a moment that feels almost too good to be true. After starting the final round trailing the field, Rose birdied the par-3 eighth hole to seize control, leaving Rory McIlroy and Cameron Young — the day's co-leaders at the start — fighting from behind.

Rose's Score
12-under par
Rose's Age
45
McIlroy Back
11-under
Young Back
10-under

McIlroy's Slip

The defending champion started Sunday tied with Cameron Young at the top of the leaderboard, fresh off his six-shot lead — a historic advantage that evaporated on Saturday's Moving Day. McIlroy's opening nine on Sunday proved fractious. A bogey on the par-3 sixth derailed his momentum, leaving him scrambling just one shot clear of Young and Russell Henley. The Northern Irishman, with one hand on his second straight Green Jacket less than two hours ago, now finds himself chasing Rose's unlikely charge.

Young's Momentum Stalls

Cameron Young arrived Sunday as co-leader but has found Augusta's back nine merciless. A bogey on the sixth, paired with Rose's relentless birdie-making, has seen him slip out of contention. Young, who finished runner-up at The Players Championship just weeks ago and was expected to mount a strong challenge, sits three strokes back with a handful of holes remaining — a gap that feels insurmountable given Rose's confidence.

Hatton Waiting in the Wings

Tyrrell Hatton signed his scorecard as clubhouse leader at 10-under par, shooting a superb final round 66 to post a total of 278. The Englishman's steady, methodical approach has him locked in at T-3, level with Young and Henley. If Rose falters on the back nine, Hatton's round — featuring two of Augusta's six 66s all week — could yet prove pivotal. The wait is on.

What's at Stake

Rose hasn't won on the PGA Tour since 2017. A Masters victory would rank among sport's greatest comeback stories — a man in his mid-40s against younger, longer hitters, triumphing on the game's most historic stage. Every shot matters now. Every putt counts. As the sun dips lower over Amen Corner, Augusta is witnessing something truly special unfold.
